summary.p: Now an English, Oxford late 19th century altered binding for the Bodleian, retaining and reusing the sewing and sewing supports of the first binding of this manuscript. The boards, endbands, and covering of the first binding are now lost and the manuscript may have been a sewn text-block within a loose manuscript wrapper during Rawlinson’s ownership. The remains of the first binding were altered for the Bodleian in the later 19th century when new boards were added and it was covered with five pieces of parchment, two of which are manuscript waste, possibly part of a Rawlinson wrapper. Although the binding has been altered it remains the first binding of the manuscript.

list.item:
First binding
An English, Salisbury, later 11th inboard binding, date and place of binding as manuscript, probably with a ‘Romanesque’ structure (cf. Szirmai, pp. 140-142); the sewing supports originally laced through oblong tunnels in the spine edge wooden boards. The binding was covered with alum-tawed skin and held closed with a single left-to-right long pin strap.
This binding, with MSS. Bodl. 392 and 444, Part 1, form a group of related Salisbury bindings with exaggerated canted spine-edge corners to their bookblocks. This canted spine-edge trim is much greater than usually found on bindings with a ‘Romanesque’ structure and is much greater than required to accommodate the endbands; although none of the three bindings are now complete, they form an identifiable group.
Although there is now no evidence of a former chain staple there are rust marks to the tail edge of the bookblock, probably from an iron chain. See MSS. Bodl. 516, 756 and 835, manuscripts with Salisbury provenance with marks from chain staples at the centre of the tail edge on one or both boards, and rust marks from an iron chain to the tail edges.

summary.p: The bookbinding is described in the order of binding using the controlled vocabulary of the Language of Bindings Thesaurus (LoB). Bookbinding description by Andrew Honey (January 2026).

binding.head: First binding: English, Salisbury, later 11th inboard binding
binding.endleaves: Left: Separate endleaves, an unfoliated blank parchment bifolium before fol. 1.
Right: Integral endleaves, the final three blank but ruled parchment leaves of quire 11 (fols. 82-84). Fol. 84, a now lifted pastedown, is conjoint with fol. 81, the final text leaf.
binding.sewing: Sewn all-along with undyed plain thread at four sewing stations on two raised spilt-strap sewing supports (10 mm wide), sewing supports now cut to the width of the spine. The Sewing stations (measured in mm from the head-edge at fols. 60v-61r): 47 (kettle), 93 (support 1), 199 (support 2), 238 (kettle), 295 (tail). There are no earlier sewing stations.
binding.boards: No longer present, though evidence of a side pin indicates wooden boards.
binding.edges: The edges of the bookblock are cut and undecorated, though the trim was minimal and some leaves retain the uneven natural cut edges of the parchment. Témoin of 4 mm at the tail/fore-edge corner of fol. 39. The bookblock has exaggerated canted spine-edge corners (8-9 mm high), much greater than required to accommodate the endbands.
binding.endbands: No longer present, though tiedowns of undyed plain thread remain at the centre of each quire and endleaves. The endband tiedowns do not always extend to the kettlestitches, indicating that they were probably sewn through thick spine linings that acted as tab stiffeners.
binding.covering: No longer present, though impressions to fol. 84v, the lifted right pastedown, indicate that the binding had a full cover of alum-tawed skin with wide turn-ins.
binding.fastenings: A hole at the centre of fol. 84, the right pastedown, caused by a side pin indicates that the binding was held closed with a single left-to-right long pin strap, now all lost.
binding.alterations: There are unexplained cut marks leaves in the same position towards the tail edge of the left endleaves and first 19 leaves (two unfoliated leaves before fols. 1, fols. 1-19).
binding.chaining: There are no stains to the endleaves from a chain staple, but rust marks to the tail edge of the bookblock probably from an iron chain.

binding.head: Alteration: English, Oxford late 19th century
binding.endleaves: Left: Separate endleaves, an unfoliated bifolium of blank wove paper added, the first leaf a pastedown.
Right: Separate endleaves, an unfoliated bifolium of blank wove paper added, the second leaf a pastedown.
binding.sewing: The manuscript retains the sewing and sewing supports of the first binding.
binding.boards: New boards were added to the surviving sewn bookblock. Paper boards with wide squares, 4.5 mm thick (including covering material), no board shaping. The boards are attached with slips added to the sewing supports as part of the alteration.
binding.edges: The edges were not trimmed as part of the alteration.
binding.spine: The spine now has a slight round shape, carried out as part of the alteration.
binding.covering: A full cover for the alteration, pieced together from plain and manuscript waste parchment, all used hairside out. Covered as a tight back and moulded at the spine and sewing supports, with tying-up cord impressions to the bands. The cover consists of five pieces, the spine is a strip of plain parchment and each board is covered with two pieces of a parchment. The larger pieces being the blank side of two parts of the same one-sided document written in English, the smaller pieces plain parchment. The manuscript waste was possibly part of a loose wrapper added for Rawlinson.

history.provenance: Salisbury, Wiltshire, Cathedral church of St Mary the Virgin; see removed from Sherborne in 1078: The same scribe s. xii wrote the letters 'D.M.' as a 'nota bene' sign in the margins of Aberdeen UL 216, Bodl. 392, 444 (fols. 1–27), 756, 765, 768, 835; Fell 1, 4; Rawl. C.723; Salisbury Cath. 10, 24, 25, 37, 67, 78, 88, 106, 128, 129, 135, 140, 154, 159, 165; cf. N. R. Ker in Wilts. Archaeol. and Nat. Hit. mag. 53 (1949), pp. 153–83 and plate. (MLGB3: evidence from marginalia, sometimes distinctive of a particular house or known scribe).
Has T. James' letter mark C. (MLGB3)

recordHist.source: Summary description abbreviated from the Quarto Catalogue (W. D. Macray, Catalogi codicum manuscriptorum Bibliothecæ Bodleianæ...viri munificentissimi Ricardi Rawlinson, J.C.D., codicum...complectens, Quarto Catalogues V, 5 fascicles, 1862–1900). Quarto Catalogue, col. 365 Summary Catalogue, vol. 3, p. 219

ref.title: MLGB3: Medieval Libraries of Great Britain

revisionDesc.change: Toby Burrows/Mapping Manuscript MigrationsProvenance and acquisition information added using https://github.com/mapping-manuscript-migrations/oxford-tei-updates/blob/master/update_oxford_prov.rb in collaboration with the Mapping Manuscript Migrations project.
James Cummings Up-converted the markup using https://github.com/jamescummings/Bodleian-msDesc-ODD/blob/master/convertTolkien2Bodley.xsl
